exemplar.title:
To what extent do tunnel vision and confirmation 
bias affect guilty verdicts in police investigations and criminal trials 
leading to false criminal convictions.

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent do tunnel vision and confirmation 
bias affect guilty verdicts in police investigations and criminal trials 
leading to false criminal convictions.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and effective communication of the topic with appropriate focus throughout
Precisely worded, sharply focused research question integrated into every section
Wellâdefined conceptual foundation with strong introduction and conclusion
weaknesses: Minor omissions in the methodology description (e.g. database search details)
Repeated misspelling of 'guilty/quilty' undermines technical precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands unequivocally meet the top band (5â6) and the methodology strand sits at the upper end of that band. The purpose and focus are clearly communicated, the research question is expertly integrated, and the review uses a coherent range of sources. The small methodological and spelling slips are insufficient to lower the overall holistic judgment, warranting the full 6 marks.

strengths: Consistent and accurate use of subject-specific terminology and concepts
Good application of a diverse range of recent, peer-reviewed sources
Clear explanations of complex concepts such as ecological validity and purposive sampling
weaknesses: Occasional factual lapses (e.g. conflating confirmation bias with racial bias)
Some source descriptions (e.g. Kunkler and Roy 2023) are too cursory
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Terminology is employed with precision and consistency, fully satisfying the top band. Subject knowledge and source application demonstrate good understanding but fall short of consistently sophisticated insight due to a few factual lapses and overly brief descriptions. Balancing these factors yields a strong 5 out of 6.

strengths: Wide variety of recent, contextually appropriate sources with clear relevance to the research question
Reasoned, structured argument linking empirical findings to real-world wrongful convictions
Strong real-world applications (e.g. linking to National Registry of Exonerations)
weaknesses: Synthesis across studies can be uneven and sometimes asserted rather than fully developed
Lack of statistical detail (p-values, effect sizes) weakens evidential support
Evaluation remains mostly descriptive without an integrative critical model of bias interactions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality is strong (band 7â9), while analysis depth and evaluation are mid-band (4â6). The overall performance is solidly above average but not yet at the expert level. A score of 8 reflects the excellent relevance of sources tempered by gaps in synthesis, statistical rigor, and depth of critical evaluation.

strengths: Fully appropriate structure with all IB conventions observed (title page, ToC, sections, page numbers)
Consistent referencing and clear layout of headings
Title and word count correctly placed at the start
weaknesses: Minor spacing and formatting inconsistencies in the table of contents
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both structural and formatting strands meet the highest band descriptors. Although there are minor spacing and typographical inconsistencies, the report adheres to IB presentation conventions throughout, justifying the top score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical terms such as **heuristics, ecological validity, quasi-random assignment, purposive sampling, conversation analysis** and **construct validity** are deployed accurately and consistently across sections.  Definitions are often supplied, and the vocabulary remains discipline-specific without major errors.  This sustained, precise use of terminology meets the descriptor for 5-6, warranting the upper score of 6.

Basic formatting requirements are met: the title and word count appear at the start, section headings are bolded, a table of contents with page numbers is provided, and pages themselves are numbered.  Referencing style is consistently applied.  Minor issues (inconsistent spacing, occasional typographical errors) keep the work from perfect polish, so a solid 3 within the 3-4 band is appropriate.

The student accurately summarises key findings from psychological research (e.g., ecological validity, sampling biases) and applies them to the argument, demonstrating **good but not consistently sophisticated knowledge**.  Occasional factual lapses (e.g., conflation of confirmation bias with âracial biasâ in places) and reliance on secondary commentary for some claims prevent the work from reaching the âexcellent/consistently relevantâ standard.  Overall performance aligns with the middle of the 3-4 band.

The student outlines explicit **inclusion/exclusion criteria** (peer-reviewed, post-2015, direct relevance to guilt verdicts) and draws on laboratory, field and case-study sources.  Around a dozen primary studies from varied jurisdictions are cited, suggesting an **appropriate range and purposeful selection**.  However, the methodology is essentially a narrative literature review with limited description of how databases were searched or how quality was systematically appraised, so evidence of *effective* informed selection is strong but not fully comprehensive.  This fits comfortably at 5 within the 5-6 band.

The title, research question and introductory paragraph immediately establish the theme of wrongful convictions and explicitly link it to the twin concepts of tunnel vision and confirmation bias.  The student explains the social importance of the problem, defines both biases, and signals how the essay will compare their influence with other biases.  Purpose and focus therefore come across as **clear, appropriate and consistently maintained** throughout the table of contents and subsequent section headings.  Minor slips in phrasing (e.g. repeated misspelling of âguilty/quiltyâ) do not impede communication, so the descriptors at the top of the 5-6 band are met.

The essay draws on a **wide variety of recent, peer-reviewed and contextually appropriate sources** (experimental, qualitative, archival) that directly address the research question.  The selection is mostly well-justified and each studyâs relevance is explained, though occasional peripheral citations (e.g., broad definitions from dictionaries) dilute the overall rigour slightly.  This places the work solidly in the 7-9 band; a score of 7 reflects good but not flawless coverage and relevance.

The research question is **precisely worded (âTo what extent do tunnel vision and confirmation bias affect guilty verdicts â¦?â)**, delimits context (police investigations and criminal trials) and is revisited in the discussion and conclusion.  Each body section is organised around answering that question, demonstrating a strong logical connection.  Hence the strand satisfies the requirement for a clearly stated, sharply focused question effectively integrated into the argument, meriting the top of the 5-6 band.

The piece follows IB conventions: title page, abstract substitute (word count), table of contents, clearly labelled sections, in-text citations and reference list.  Logical progression from introduction through studies, discussion and conclusion is evident.  All required structural elements are present and appropriately sequenced, satisfying the descriptor for 3-4 marks; the execution is smooth enough to merit the top of that band.

The discussion section offers a **reasoned argument** summarising how tunnel vision and confirmation bias interact with other biases, and considers cultural variation.  Evaluation of construct validity and limitations is present but remains mostly descriptive, and the essay stops short of a truly integrative critical model explaining relative weightings of biases.  The argument is structured and partially critical, matching the upper half of the 4-6 band; a score of 5 is therefore justified.

Analysis follows each study synopsis with a paragraph on **strengths, limitations and implications**, demonstrating understanding of methodological issues (sample size, ecological validity, demand characteristics).  Nevertheless, synthesis across studies is uneven; comparisons are sometimes asserted rather than developed and statistical reasoning is absent.  Conclusions are usually supported but not always fully evidenced.  These qualities match the middle of the 4-6 band, so 5 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: Word count is clearly provided and remains under the 4000-word limit, satisfying formatting requirements and transparency.
Consider introducing formal definitions of heuristics and biases before applying them, to scaffold reader understanding.
The title succinctly identifies the focus on tunnel vision and confirmation bias in wrongful convictions, effectively framing the investigationâs scope and purpose.
Repeated misspelling of âquiltyâ instead of âguiltyâ undermines precision; correct technical terms improve clarity and professional tone.
Table of contents lists all sections with accurate page numbers, aiding navigability and demonstrating coherent structure.
TOC table formatting appears cramped in places; consider adjusting row spacing and column widths for readability.
Introduction effectively contextualises wrongful convictions with statistics and impacts, establishing a compelling rationale.
Sample description in Elaad (2022) is thorough, clearly differentiating investigator and layperson groups and experimental tasks.
Results discussion lacks statistical details (e.g., p-values, effect sizes), which would strengthen the evidential basis of the claims.
Explanation of pre-trial bias snowballing is clear and conceptually insightful for understanding compounding effects.
Discussion of construct validity is descriptive; integrate theoretical frameworks or meta-analysis insights to deepen evaluation.
Comparison across multiple cultural contexts shows high critical engagement and broadens applicability of findings.
To clarify bias interactions, propose a conceptual model or diagram illustrating how biases reinforce one another.
Reflection on bias interactions in limitations section exhibits evaluative depth but lacks personal insight into research process.
Consider adding a brief reflection on methodological decisions (e.g., source inclusion choices) to strengthen personal engagement.
Conclusion concisely reiterates research question and key arguments, effectively closing the investigation.
Policy recommendation remains broad; specify concrete mitigation strategies (e.g., blind evidence review protocols).
References include a diverse range of recent, peer-reviewed sources, demonstrating strong subject knowledge.
Definitions of tunnel vision and confirmation bias are clear and wellâintegrated, laying a solid conceptual foundation.
Methodology section omits details on which databases were searched and what keywords were used; add specifics for replicability.
Evaluation of Elaad (2022) highlights internal validity strengths, including GPower analysis, reflecting sound critical thinking.
To improve generalisability, propose a stratified random sampling approach reflecting diverse law enforcement roles.
Description of Kunkler and Roy (2023) is too cursory; elaborate on their empirical findings and practical recommendations.
Integration of Edgcumbeâs large sample and attention-check measures demonstrates strong understanding of methodological rigour.
Caseâstudy application in Maynard and Schelly (2017) enhances ecological validity by linking theory to real interactions.
Qualitative analysis may be affected by researcher bias; consider triangulating with physical evidence or third-party transcripts.
Raceâcrime congruency effect is well-explained, showing clear experimental design and theoretical linkage.
Convenience sampling from universities limits external validity; discuss ways to mitigate sample bias in such studies.
Linking experimental results to National Registry of Exonerations data provides compelling real-world relevance.
Include deeper critique of purposive sampling in Sorby and Kehn (2021), noting potential researcher influence on conversation selection.
Definition of ethnic bias is clear and well-situated, effectively connecting concept to judicial contexts.
Choi et al.âs statistical models are mentioned but not detailed; discuss model type and control variables to strengthen analysis.
Large genuine case dataset in Kenyan appeal study substantially enhances ecological validity and real-world relevance.
Consider acknowledging intersecting factors like socioeconomic status and gender, which may compound or mitigate ethnic bias.
Some reference entries exhibit inconsistent title capitalization and DOI formatting; standardise APA style throughout.
